rulings.Case Title: KAI KOPP VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant San Francisco Latin American Baseball Leagues Notice Of Demurrer To Plaintiffs Second Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, September 5, 2018, Line 3, Defendant San Francisco Latin American Baseball Leagues' Demurrer To Plaintiffs Second Amended Complaint Defendant San Francisco Latin American Baseball League's demurrer to the second and third cause of action in the second amended complaint filed by plaintiff Kai Kopp is overruled as to the second cause of action for negligence and sustained without leave to amend as to the third cause of action for negligent supervision, hiring and training. The negligence cause of action, but not the negligent supervision cause of action, is based on the same general set of facts as the complaint. Both the complaint and second cause of action in second amended complaint allege negligence with regard to the location of the light pole. The third cause of action in the second amended complaint is based on alleged negligence with regard to the supervision and conduct of the umpires which is nowhere mentioned in the complaint. Since the third cause of action does not relate back to the filing of the complaint, it is time-barred. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the League is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =3.2/HK

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 5, 2018, Line 1, INTERVENORS LEE WEINBERG, UNITED STATES LIABILITY INSURANCE COMPANY'S Motion To Compel Saison Group Llc To Provide A Verified Written Response Containing No Objections And Produce Responsive Documents Responsive To Intervenors Demand For Production Of Documents (Set One), And For An Order That Saison Group Llc Must Pay Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Chuck Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted, no opposition. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to cgeerhart@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: BRENDA GISI VS. HUDDLE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Huddle Inc'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, September 5, 2018, Line 6, PART 2 of 2 TENTATIVE RULINGS, Defendant Huddle Inc'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication Huddle is entitled to summary adjudication of the retaliation claims because the undisputed evidence shows there is no causal connection between Ms. Gisi's protected activity and her termination. The court credits Mr. Roberts' declaration that he was not aware that Ms. Gisi had complained that about his discriminatory treatment of Ms. Gisi and the older employees. Mr. Roberts made the decision to terminate Ms. Gisi's employment before Huddle received Ms. Gisi's email complaining of age discrimination. There is insufficient circumstantial evidence to negate Mr. Roberts' declaration regarding his lack of knowledge of Ms. Gisi's protected activity. The doctrine of imputed knowledge is not a part of California retaliation law. The court declines to deny summary adjudication on the retaliation claims per CCP 437c(e) because there is no basis to disbelieve Mr. Roberts' declaration regarding his lack of knowledge of Ms. Gisi's protected activity. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Gisi is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/HK

rulings.Case Title: BRENDA GISI VS. HUDDLE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, September 5, 2018, Line 6, Part 1 of 2 tentative rulings, DEFENDANT HUDDLE, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication After review of the parties' supplemental papers, the court remains of the view that Hudddle is entitled to summary adjudication in its favor on the retaliation claims and thus re-affirms the tentative ruling issued prior to the July 26, 2018 hearing with some additional language as follows: Defendant Huddle, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternative motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the third, fifth, sixth, and eighth causes of action for age discrimination, failure to prevent discrimination, and violation of the UCL and granted as to the fourth and seventh causes of action for retaliation. Ms. Gisi satisfied her light burden of stating a prima facie case of age discrimination by showing that there are circumstances suggesting a discriminatory motive such as Mr. Roberts' remarks about what kind of people he needed and Ms. Gisi's accounts were distributed to team members significantly younger than her. Ms. Gisi also satisfied her burden of showing that Huddle's proffered legitimate reason for terminating Ms. Gisi (the need to lay off employees with poor performance) was pretextual based on the following evidence liberally construed in her favor: (1) the remarks of Mr. Brogger to Ms. Gisi's former supervisor that, while he wanted to retain Ms. Herrington who was "young and pretty and energetic," he wondered whether she had gone "to the dinosaur graveyard to find [her] team" of which Ms. Gisi was a member; (2) Mr. Roberts' remarks to Ms. Herrington that he needed "'fast ¿sharp people . . . with a clean slate'. . .and not burdened with years of experience elsewhere" (3) Mr. Roberts' differential treatment of the older sales representatives compared to the younger ones; (4) inconsistencies in Huddle's explanations as to how and when the decision to terminate plaintiff was made; and (5) there was a significant change of focus of Ms. Gisi's work which negatively affected her ability to achieve her sales quota. This evidence and Huddle's failure to take action in response to Ms. Gisi's complaint to Ms. Murray precludes granting summary adjudication on the third, fifth, sixth and eighth causes of action. (Reid v. Google, Inc. (2010) 50 Cal.4th 512, 541 (rejecting the "stray remarks" doctrine because "[d]etermining the weight of discriminatory or ambiguous remarks is a role reserved for the jury.")).

rulings.Case Title: DAVID DONOHUE VS. GEORGE HENRY PFLEGER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Confirming The Legal Right To Review Unredacted Medical Records, Permitting Defendant To Subpoena Marriage Counselors For Appearance At Depositions With Production Of Documents On Shortened Notice Permitting Defendant To Subpoena Marriage Counselors Records On Shortened Notice Permitting Discovery Beyond Discovery Cut-Off Date Shortening Notice On Motions Relating To The Identity Of Marriage Counselors And Marriage
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, September 5, 2018, Line 9, DEFENDANT GEORGE PFLEGER's Motion For An Order Confirming The Legal Right To Review Unredacted Medical Records, Permitting Defendant To Subpoena Marriage Counselors For Appearance At Depositions With Production Of Documents On Shortened Notice Permitting Defendant To Subpoena Marriage Counselors Records On Shortened Notice Permitting Discovery Beyond Discovery Cut-Off Date Shortening Notice On Motions Relating To The Identity Of Marriage Counselors And Marriage Defendant George Pfleger's motion for permission to receive the names of the Donohues' marriage counselors and other requests regarding the marriage counselors is denied in its entirety. Mr. Pfleger has not provided a sufficient showing of need for any of the discovery he seeks to overcome the privacy and privilege objections asserted by Kathleen Donohue. Simply asserting that the requested discovery is critical without showing what efforts, if any, were made to obtain the assertedly necessary information from other sources is not sufficient to override Ms. Donohue's important privacy interests. For instance, Mr. Pfleger does not state why an IME, the depositions of the Ms. Donohue, plaintiff David Donohue, and Mr. Donohue's medical and therapeutic providers and/or the records from those providers is insufficient to enable him to evaluate and respond to Mr. Donohue's contention that his marriage failed as a result of the accident. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Donohue is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=302/hk

rulings.Case Title: BROADBAND ITV, INC. VS. OPENTV, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, September 5, 2018, Line 8, PLAINTIFF BROADBAND ITV, INC.'s Motion For Protective Order Plaintiff Broadband ITV, Inc.'s motion for protective order is granted in part. The parties are required to meet and confer in an effort to agree on a protective order that: 1) provides for two levels of confidential information called "Highly Confidential" and "Confidential"; 2) either side may designate any information as either Highly Confidential or Confidential subject to a motion by the other side challenging that designation; 3) both Highly Confidential and Confidential Information may be used by the receiving party only for this case and must be returned at the conclusion of the case with a declaration from an attorney stating that all such information has been returned; 4) all information designated Highly Confidential may only be provided to the attorneys, one outside expert and one person affiliated with the receiving party, which can include Mr. Diaz in the case of Broadband; 5) defendant OpenTV, Inc. is not required to provide any source code to Broadband; 6) all information designated Confidential may only be provided to persons who are working on this case for the receiving party; 7) prior to receiving any information designated Highly Confidential the person receiving such information must identify in a declaration filed under seal in this case with the specificity required for a CCP 2019.210 disclosure all non-public information known to that person that in any way relates to the subject matter of the Highly Confidential information; and 8) no person receiving any information designated Highly Confidential or Confidential is subject to any patent prosecution bar. A further hearing will be held on September 26, 2018 in the event that the parties are not able to agree on the language of a protective order in accordance with this ruling before that date. OpenTV's cross-motion for a protective order is moot and taken off calendar. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Broadband is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=302/hk
